sentences.base_language: I just got some great news!
sentences.romanized: Wǒ gāng shōudào yī gè hǎo xiāoxi!
sentences.literal: I just received one good news!
sentences.grammarNote: 刚 (gāng) = 'just (did something a moment ago)'. It implies very recent completion. 收到 = 'received' (收 = receive, 到 = resultative complement indicating success).

sentences.romanized: Wǒ dédào nà fèn gōngzuò le!
sentences.grammarNote: 得到 (dédào) = 'to obtain/get'. 了 at the end of the sentence marks a change of state — something has now become true that wasn't before. It's the 'new situation' 了.

sentences.base_language: I'm so happy I could cry.
sentences.grammarNote: 得 (de) as a degree complement: adjective + 得 + result/degree. '高兴得想哭' = 'so happy (that I) want to cry'. This 得 is different from 的 (possessive/modifier) and 地 (adverbial). Tone and character differ!

sentences.base_language: My whole family is very proud of me.
sentences.target_language: 我全家人都为我感到骄傲。
sentences.romanized: Wǒ quán jiārén dōu wèi wǒ gǎndào jiāo'ào.
sentences.literal: My whole family all for me feel proud.
sentences.grammarNote: 都 (dōu) = 'all / both'. It requires a plural or collective subject: 全家人都 (the whole family all…). 都 always comes before the verb. 为 here = 'for / on behalf of': 为你高兴 (happy for you).
应该 (yīnggāi) = 'should / ought to'. Modal verbs in Chinese come before the main verb, same position as 想, 要, 能, 可以. 一下 softens: 'celebrate a bit / have a little celebration'.

sentences.base_language: Let's go to dinner to celebrate.
sentences.romanized: Wǒmen qù chī dùn fàn qìngzhù ba.
sentences.literal: We go eat a meal to celebrate [suggestion].
sentences.grammarNote: 顿 (dùn) is the measure word for meals: 一顿饭 (a meal). 吃顿饭 = 'eat a meal'. The purpose clause 庆祝 (celebrate) comes after the main action, unlike English where it might come first.

sentences.base_language: This is the best day of my life.
sentences.target_language: 这是我人生中最美好的一天。
sentences.romanized: Zhè shì wǒ rénshēng zhōng zuì měihǎo de yītiān.
sentences.literal: This is my life's most wonderful day.

sentences.base_language: I worked really hard for this.
sentences.target_language: 我为这件事付出了很多努力。
sentences.romanized: Wǒ wèi zhè jiàn shì fùchū le hěn duō nǔlì.
sentences.literal: I for this matter put-in many efforts.
sentences.grammarNote: 件 (jiàn) is the measure word for matters, affairs, clothing items: 一件事 (a matter), 一件衬衫 (a shirt). 付出 = 'to put in (effort)'. The 了 here marks the completed effort.
